pride-and-prejudice.title: Pride and Prejudice
pride-and-prejudice.text: Jane Austen originally titled the novel First Impressions. It became one of the most loved classics in English literature and is famous for Elizabeth Bennet and Mr. Darcy.

1984.text: George Orwell wrote 1984 as a warning about surveillance, propaganda, and authoritarian control. Terms like Big Brother and thoughtcrime became widely known because of this novel.

the-hobbit.title: The Hobbit
the-hobbit.text: The Hobbit was first written as a story for Tolkien's children. It later became the entry point to the larger world of Middle-earth.

little-women.title: Little Women
little-women.text: Louisa May Alcott drew from her own family life when writing Little Women, and the character of Jo is often seen as inspired by Alcott herself.

the-book-thief.title: The Book Thief
the-book-thief.text: The Book Thief is narrated by Death, which gives the novel its unusual and memorable storytelling voice.

jane-austen.title: Jane Austen
jane-austen.text: Jane Austen published her novels anonymously during her lifetime. Her sharp observations about class, relationships, and manners made her one of the most influential novelists in English literature.

george-orwell.title: George Orwell
george-orwell.text: George Orwell was the pen name of Eric Arthur Blair. He is especially known for Animal Farm and 1984, both of which explore power and political control.

tolkien.title: J.R.R. Tolkien
tolkien.text: Tolkien was not only a novelist but also a philologist. He created entire languages for the world of Middle-earth before finishing many of its stories.

harper-lee.title: Harper Lee
harper-lee.text: Harper Lee's To Kill a Mockingbird became an instant classic and won the Pulitzer Prize. For many years it was the only novel she published.

louisa-may-alcott.title: Louisa May Alcott
louisa-may-alcott.text: Louisa May Alcott first hesitated to write Little Women, but it became her most famous work and a lasting favorite across generations.
